text:
KINGS NEUROSCIENCES CENTRE


Clinic Date: 02 October 2025 

Dr Alice Patel
Riverside Medical Centre

221 Thames View, London, SE10 3AB
Dear Dr Alice Patel
Jane Smith, DOB: 21-11-1982, Hospital No: H829374 NHS No. 9473628190
12A Brookside Close, London, SE10 1PX 

Thank you for asking me to review the above patient in the Neurology Clinic today. She is followed under our service with a diagnosis of focal epilepsy. Since I last saw her, she reports a deterioration in day-to-day function that appears to be temporally related to a change in her working environment. Specifically, her new workplace uses strong and intermittently flickering overhead lighting and display screens; she and her manager have both noted that more events and near-events seem to cluster on days spent in that setting.

Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every two - three months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every six months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes. While this pattern has remained numerically unchanged, she describes increased prodromal symptoms at work (eye strain, nausea, and a sense of dissociation) and more frequent brief spells of lost concentration when exposed to the lighting, which she and her partner worry may herald further focal seizures. She has also curtailed hours and is avoiding certain meeting rooms due to perceived triggers, which is impacting her employment and wellbeing.

Current antiseizure therapy is Lamotrigine 150 mg b.d. and Levetiracetam 1 g b.d., both tolerated without rash or mood change; she notes intermittent fatigue by late afternoon. She denies new systemic symptoms, intercurrent illness, missed doses, excess alcohol, or sleep deprivation beyond baseline. There is no recent head injury. Family have not witnessed convulsions since the last appointment, but they corroborate the increase in warning symptoms under bright or flickering lights. She does not drive.

Neurological examination today was non-focal. Routine bloods from last month (FBC, U&Es, LFTs) were within expected limits per GP letter. She has not yet had an occupational health assessment at her new workplace.

In summary, she has focal epilepsy with a stable recorded seizure frequency, but there is a clear clinical concern that environmental photic stimuli at work are provoking more premonitory events and apparent absences, and her functional status is deteriorating as a result. I have advised meticulous seizure diary keeping with annotation of lighting exposure and screen use, and she will liaise with her employer regarding reasonable adjustments (reduced flicker settings on displays, higher refresh-rate monitors, glare filters, consistent ambient lighting, and avoidance of strobing fixtures). We discussed stress management and regular sleep as supportive measures. I will arrange an EEG with photic stimulation and an occupational health letter describing the likely role of lighting as a trigger and the need for adjustments.

I will review her again in three months, sooner if she or you are concerned. Please encourage her to seek urgent advice if she experiences clusters, escalating frequency, or injury.

Yours sincerely

Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every six months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes

check__Seizure Frequency Number.analysis: Identify explicit frequencies: (1) Generalised tonic-clonic: "one ... every two - three months" and "maximum seizure-free period of four weeks". Converting 2–3 months gives 1 per 2 to 3 months. The 'maximum seizure-free period of four weeks' implies at least 1 per month at times, but the explicit frequency stated is every 2–3 months; we keep the explicit numeric expression. (2) Weekly absences persist → absences occur weekly = 1 per week. (3) Atonic seizures: once every six months = 1 per 6 months. (4) Focal seizures with impaired awareness: once every six months = 1 per 6 months. Rule: choose the highest frequency (most frequent). Weekly absences (1 per week) are more frequent than monthly or biannual events. No evidence of 6-month seizure freedom; not seizure-free. Therefore final label: 1 per week. Quote the relevant phrase.
reference: Over the past six months seizure control has been stable; she suffers one generalised tonic-clonic seizure every two - three months, with a maximum seizure-free period of four weeks. Weekly absences persist. She also experiences atonic seizures and focal seizures with impaired awareness, each arising once every six months. Since the last visit, she has not had any reported episodes
